text: Greetings from Jitma Puducherry. The technique of robotic right hepatectomy with caudate lobectomy for hilar cholangiocarcinoma is described in this video. Author declares no conflict of interest.

Major hepatectomy for high-law cholangiocarcinoma is one of the most challenging abdominal procedure, hence minimally invasive approach is not commonly used. The previous case reports on minimally invasive approach for high-law cholangiocarcinoma, the right hepatectomy and caudate lobectomy were performed separately. The technique of robotic right hepatectomy with on-block caudate lobectomy is described here.

A fifty-year-old male, present with jaundice and pleuritis of two months duration, with a total bilirubin of twenty-six milligrams per deciliter and CN-Nitinine of five hundred and forty-two international units. The CT abdomen showed a type III hyaluronucleotide carcinoma with right hepatic artery involvement. Hence, a PTBD of left and right posterior sectoral duct was done. and the PTBD gram done through left hepatic duct and right posterior sectoral duct confirmed type IIIa AHC.

After optimization, the patient was planned for a surgery. The future liver remnant was adequate. The procedure was done using Da Vinci X-ray robotic system. The patient was positioned supine with leg split.

The surgeon uses four robotic and two assistant trocars to perform the procedure. After retracting the liver, lymphadenectomy begins with segmented ATL lymph node dissection. The dissection continues along the left side of the hepatoduodenal ligament towards the TLA, followed by TLP dissection. The right gastric artery is clipped and divided, and lymphadenectomy proceeds along the right side of the hepatoduodenal ligament. The cholecystectomy is completed, aiding further lymph node dissection.

The surgeon divides the bile duct and sends the lower bile duct margin for frozen section evaluation. Further dissection reveals the tumor at the level of the hilum. The right hepatic artery is dissected, looped, and divided. Subsequently, the portal vein is looped to facilitate the dissection and isolation of the right branch of the portal vein.

The surgeon loops and ligates the right branch of the portal vein, applying a hemolog clip before dividing it. The right liver is then mobilized, and multiple short hepatic veins draining the caudate lobe are divided after being ligated with hemolyclips. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to identify the demarcation line between the right and left lobes, which is marked with a cautery.

The surgeon performs liver transection using a combination of bipolar and monopolar devices. The Pringle maneuver is intermittently applied to minimize blood loss and aid in the dissection and identification of segmental hepatic veins draining into the middle hepatic vein. Dissection is carried out at the level of the hilum plate, and the bile duct is clamped and divided at the level of the left hepatic duct.

The surgeon mobilizes the left portion of the caudate lobe, controlling a large hepatic vein branch draining the superior aspect with bulldog clamps. This allows for complete separation of the caudate from the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the left lobe. The caudate is then mobilized to the right of the portal vein and IVC, facilitating an on-block caudate lobectomy.

The surgeon continues the liver transection cranially. The right hepatic vein is looped and divided using a vascular stapler, completing the right hepatectomy. A Roux limb of jejunum is transferred to the supracolic compartment, and a hepatico-jejunostomy is performed between the left hepatic duct and the jejunum using four polydioxanone sutures.

The surgeon completes the hepatic vaginostomy and performs a bile leak test to ensure no anastomotic leak is present. Hemostatic powders are applied to the liver cut surface. The specimen shows the right hepatectomy with en bloc caudate lobectomy.

The patient had an uneventful post-operative course and discharged on the seventh post-operative day and the histopathology revealed T to be tumoured and all lymph nodes are free of tumour. Robotic right haplectomy with on-block caudic lobectomy for HILAR cholangiocarcinoma is safe and feasible and robotic approach might be the preferred minimally invasive approach for HILAR cholangiocarcinoma.
